title: श्री शिव पञ्चरत्न स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शिव पञ्चरत्न स्तोत्रम् ॥

॥ श्री शंकराचार्यकृतम् ॥

नागेन्द्रहाराय त्रिलोचनाय
भस्माङ्गरागाय महेश्वराय।
नित्याय शुद्धाय दिगम्बराय
तस्मै 'न'काराय नमः शिवाय॥ १॥

मन्दाकिनीसलिलचन्दनचर्चिताय
नन्दीश्वरप्रमथनाथमहेश्वराय।
मन्दारपुष्पबहुपुष्पसुपूजिताय
तस्मै 'म'काराय नमः शिवाय॥ २॥

शिवाय गौरीवदनाब्जवृन्द-
सूर्याय दक्षाध्वरनाशकाय।
श्रीनीलकण्ठाय वृषध्वजाय
तस्मै 'शि'काराय नमः शिवाय॥ ३॥

वशिष्ठकुम्भोद्भवगौतमार्य-
मुनीन्द्रदेवार्चितशेखराय।
चन्द्रार्कवैश्वानरलोचनाय
तस्मै 'व'काराय नमः शिवाय॥ ४॥

यक्षस्वरूपाय जटाधराय
पिनाकहस्ताय सनातनाय।
दिव्याय देवाय दिगम्बराय
तस्मै 'य'काराय नमः शिवाय॥ ५॥

पञ्चाक्षरमिदं पुण्यं यः पठेच्छिवसन्निधौ।
शिवलोकमवाप्नोति शिवेन सह मोदते॥

॥ इति श्रीमच्छंकराचार्यकृतं शिवपञ्चरत्नस्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shiva Pancharatna Stotram ||

|| Shri Shankaracharya-kritam ||

Nagendra-haraya Trilochanaya
Bhasmanga-ragaya Maheshwaraya |
Nityaya Shuddhaya Digambaraya
Tasmai 'Na'-karaya Namah Shivaya || 1 ||

Mandakini-salila-chandana-charchitaya
Nandishwara-pramatha-natha-Maheshwaraya |
Mandara-pushpa-bahu-pushpa-supujitaya
Tasmai 'Ma'-karaya Namah Shivaya || 2 ||

Shivaya Gauri-vadana-abja-vrinda-
Suryaya Daksha-adhvara-nashakaya |
Shri-Nilakanthaya Vrishadhvajaya
Tasmai 'Shi'-karaya Namah Shivaya || 3 ||

Vashishtha-Kumbhodbhava-Gautamarya-
Munindra-Devarchita-shekharaya |
Chandarka-Vaishvanara-lochanaya
Tasmai 'Va'-karaya Namah Shivaya || 4 ||

Yaksha-svarupaya Jatadharaya
Pinaka-hastaya Sanatanaya |
Divyaya Devaya Digambaraya
Tasmai 'Ya'-karaya Namah Shivaya || 5 ||

Panchaksharam-idam punyam yah pathech-Chiva-sannidhau |
Shivalokam-avapnoti Shivena saha modate ||

|| Iti Shrimach-Chankaracharyakritam Shiva-Pancharatna-Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: नागेन्द्रहार धारी, त्रिलोचन, भस्मांगराग, महेश्वर, नित्य, शुद्ध, दिगम्बर — 'न' कार रूप शिव को नमस्कार। मन्दाकिनी जल और चन्दन से चर्चित, नन्दीश्वर-प्रमथनाथ-महेश्वर, मन्दार और बहुपुष्पों से पूजित — 'म' कार रूप शिव को नमस्कार। गौरी के मुखकमल के सूर्य, दक्षयज्ञनाशक, नीलकण्ठ, वृषभध्वज शिव — 'शि' कार रूप को नमस्कार। वशिष्ठ, अगस्त्य, गौतम आदि मुनीन्द्रों और देवताओं द्वारा अर्चित, चन्द्र-सूर्य-अग्नि लोचन — 'व' कार रूप शिव को नमस्कार। यक्षस्वरूप, जटाधर, पिनाकहस्त, सनातन, दिव्य, दिगम्बर — 'य' कार रूप शिव को नमस्कार। इस प्रकार 'नमः शिवाय' पञ्चाक्षर मन्त्र के प्रत्येक अक्षर की महिमा गाई गई है।
benefits: Explains the mystical significance of each letter of Namah Shivaya
Invokes shivaloka and eternal joy with lord shiva
Composed by Adi Shankaracharya - carries immense spiritual power
Traditionally recited for removal of sins when recited in shiva's presence
Invokes the blessing of the complete fruit of panchakshari mantra
Ideal daily prayer for all Shiva devotees
description: Dedicated to Lord Shiva, the Pancha Ratna Stotram (श्री शिव पञ्चरत्न स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Shiva Pancharatna Stotram by Adi Shankaracharya, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to grant Shivaloka and eternal joy with Lord Shiva as well as destroy sins when recited in Shiva's presence. Additionally, it is known to bestow the complete fruit of Panchakshari mantra. This prayer is traditionally recited on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar to invoke divine grace. With 5 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Pancha Ratna Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shiva Pancharatna Stotram by Adi Shankaracharya in Sanskrit with transliteration. Five gems on Namah Shivaya Panchakshari mantra.
source: Shiva Pancharatna Stotram by Adi Shankaracharya

seoFaqs.question: What does Pancha Ratna Stotram mean?
seoFaqs.answer: Pancha Ratna literally means 'five jewels' (pancha = five, ratna = gem), referring to a hymn structured in exactly five Sanskrit verses, each considered a precious jewel of devotion. The Pancharatna form is one of the most beloved templates used by Adi Shankaracharya, who composed Pancharatna stotras for several deities including Ganesha, Devi, Lalita, and Shiva. The brevity of five verses makes them suitable for daily recitation, while their poetic density delivers complete philosophical and devotional meaning. (Source: Hinduism Outlook on Ganesha Pancharatnam; KKSF USA lecture notes.)

seoFaqs.question: Which Pancharatna Stotram is this and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Pancharatna Stotram tradition is most strongly associated with Jagadguru Adi Shankaracharya (8th century CE), who composed five-verse hymns to multiple deities. Among the most widely chanted are the Maha Ganesha Pancharatnam, the Lalitha Pancharatnam (to Lalita Tripurasundari), and the Devi Pancharatnam. Each hymn condenses the deity's iconography, theological significance, and grace-bearing power into five tightly composed verses. The exact deity addressed depends on the specific recension; the format itself signals Shankara's signature hand. (Source: Devshoppe; KKSF USA Ganesa Pancharathnam Lecture Notes.)

seoFaqs.question: Why did Adi Shankaracharya prefer the five-verse format?
seoFaqs.answer: Adi Shankara composed devotional hymns alongside his Advaita Vedanta commentaries to make spiritual practice accessible to all devotees, not merely to scholars. The five-verse format reflects multiple symbolic associations in Hindu tradition: the five elements (pancha-bhuta), the five sheaths of the self (pancha-kosha), the five faces of Shiva, and the five sense-organs. By compressing praise into five verses, Shankara created hymns short enough for daily japa yet rich enough to carry the full theological weight of the deity invoked. (Source: Hinduism Outlook; traditional Smarta commentary.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Pancha Ratna Stotram be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Most Pancharatna stotras are short enough to be chanted at the beginning or end of daily puja, before meals, or at the conclusion of japa with a 108-bead mala. The Ganesha Pancharatnam is traditionally recited at the start of any auspicious activity. The Devi Pancharatnam is chanted during Navaratri, especially on Ashtami and Navami. The Lalitha Pancharatnam serves as the morning prayer that opens the day's recitation of the Lalita Sahasranama. Daily morning recitation after a bath is considered ideal. (Source: Wise Ganesha; Sanatan Veda; Devshoppe.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits do devotees receive from chanting Pancharatna stotras?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional commentaries on Adi Shankara's Pancharatna hymns state that disciplined recitation bestows good health, wealth, knowledge, and the inner stillness suitable for jnana sadhana. The Ganesha Pancharatnam is said to remove obstacles to learning and undertakings; the Lalitha and Devi Pancharatnams are recited for shakti, prosperity, and protection of the household. Because the verses come from the pen of an enlightened acharya, they are believed to carry mantric power beyond their literal meaning. (Source: Hinduism Outlook; Wise Ganesha; KKSF USA notes.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Pancharatna Stotram different from the Carnatic Pancharatna Kritis?
seoFaqs.answer: The two should not be confused. The Sanskrit Pancharatna Stotras are devotional hymns by Adi Shankaracharya structured in five verses each. The Pancharatna Kritis are five compositions by the Carnatic music saint Tyagaraja (1767-1847), composed in Telugu and Sanskrit and dedicated to Lord Rama, sung annually at the Tyagaraja Aradhana in Tiruvaiyaru. Both share the 'five jewels' name but belong to different genres, languages, traditions, and centuries. (Source: Carnatic music tradition; Smarta stotra anthologies.)
